title: Science
subtitle: Week 2: Asking Questions Like a Scientist [Advanced]

slides.title: Formulating Scientific Questions (Advanced)
slides.text:
An advanced scientific question must isolate variables and be testable through empirical observation and controlled experimentation.
This slide introduces key insights about Formulating Scientific Questions.
For instance, consider the example of Empirical:
Based on verifiable observations or experience rather than theory.
Another case is the study of Isolating Variables:
Ensuring only one factor is changed to observe its direct effect.
How does the concept of Formulating Scientific Questions help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Empirical
examples.content: Based on verifiable observations or experience rather than theory.

examples.title: Isolating Variables
examples.content: Ensuring only one factor is changed to observe its direct effect.

slides.title: Inquiry and Hypotheses (Advanced)
slides.text:
Questions drive hypotheses.
A hypothesis acts as a bridge between a question and an experimental test.
For instance, consider the example of Formulating:
Converting 'Why does this happen?' into 'If we change X, then Y will behave in this way.'
Another case is the study of Operational Definition:
Defining exactly how a variable will be measured.
How does the concept of Inquiry and Hypotheses help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Formulating
examples.content: Converting 'Why does this happen?' into 'If we change X, then Y will behave in this way.'

examples.title: Operational Definition
examples.content: Defining exactly how a variable will be measured.

slides.title: Defining Testable Parameters (Advanced)
slides.text:
Vague concepts like 'better', 'faster', or 'stronger' must be defined using measurable parameters (time, length, mass).
This slide introduces key insights about Defining Testable Parameters.
For instance, consider the example of Subjective:
Example: 'Which plane is best?' (Not testable).
Another case is the study of Objective:
Example: 'Which plane design has the longest flight time in seconds?' (Testable).
How does the concept of Defining Testable Parameters help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Subjective
examples.content: Example: 'Which plane is best?' (Not testable).

examples.title: Objective
examples.content: Example: 'Which plane design has the longest flight time in seconds?' (Testable).

slides.title: Tinkering with Viscosity (Advanced)
slides.text:
Tinkering with different liquids (water, oil, honey) shows how liquid thickness affects flow rate.
This slide introduces key insights about Tinkering with Viscosity.
For instance, consider the example of Viscosity:
A fluid's resistance to flow. Honey has higher viscosity than water.
Another case is the study of Experiment:
How does heating honey affect its flow rate down a cardboard ramp?
How does the concept of Tinkering with Viscosity help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Viscosity
examples.content: A fluid's resistance to flow. Honey has higher viscosity than water.

examples.title: Experiment
examples.content: How does heating honey affect its flow rate down a cardboard ramp?

slides.title: Tinkering with Air Pressure (Advanced)
slides.text:
Tinkering with syringes and plastic tubes shows how gas compression behaves under force.
This slide introduces key insights about Tinkering with Air Pressure.
For instance, consider the example of Compression:
Squeezing air decreases its volume while increasing pressure.
Another case is the study of Pneumatics:
Using compressed gas to transmit force and perform mechanical work.
How does the concept of Tinkering with Air Pressure help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Compression
examples.content: Squeezing air decreases its volume while increasing pressure.

examples.title: Pneumatics
examples.content: Using compressed gas to transmit force and perform mechanical work.

slides.title: Tinkering with Capillary Action (Advanced)
slides.text:
Tinkering with paper towels and colored water shows how liquids climb upwards against gravity.
This slide introduces key insights about Tinkering with Capillary Action.
For instance, consider the example of Capillary Action:
Liquid moving through tiny spaces due to adhesion and cohesion.
Another case is the study of Application:
How does paper towel thickness affect the rate of water absorption?
How does the concept of Tinkering with Capillary Action help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Capillary Action
examples.content: Liquid moving through tiny spaces due to adhesion and cohesion.

examples.title: Application
examples.content: How does paper towel thickness affect the rate of water absorption?

slides.title: Tinkering with Electromagnetism (Advanced)
slides.text:
Tinkering with copper wire, an iron nail, and a battery creates a temporary magnet.
This slide introduces key insights about Tinkering with Electromagnetism.
For instance, consider the example of Electromagnet:
A magnet created by running electric current through a coil of wire.
Another case is the study of Variables:
How does the number of wire coils affect the number of paperclips pulled?
How does the concept of Tinkering with Electromagnetism help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Electromagnet
examples.content: A magnet created by running electric current through a coil of wire.

examples.title: Variables
examples.content: How does the number of wire coils affect the number of paperclips pulled?

slides.title: Tinkering with Aerodynamic Lift (Advanced)
slides.text:
Tinkering with curved cardboard wings demonstrates Bernoulli's principle of air velocity and pressure.
This slide introduces key insights about Tinkering with Aerodynamic Lift.
For instance, consider the example of Lift:
Upward aerodynamic force created by pressure differences on a wing.
Another case is the study of Angle of Attack:
The tilt of the wing relative to oncoming air flow.
How does the concept of Tinkering with Aerodynamic Lift help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Lift
examples.content: Upward aerodynamic force created by pressure differences on a wing.

examples.title: Angle of Attack
examples.content: The tilt of the wing relative to oncoming air flow.

slides.title: Tinkering with Surface Tension (Advanced)
slides.text:
Tinkering with water, pepper flakes, and liquid dish soap shows how surface molecules stick together.
This slide introduces key insights about Tinkering with Surface Tension.
For instance, consider the example of Surface Tension:
Cohesive forces that make water act like it has an elastic skin.
Another case is the study of Surfactant:
Soap breaks surface tension, causing pepper to scatter instantly.
How does the concept of Tinkering with Surface Tension help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Surface Tension
examples.content: Cohesive forces that make water act like it has an elastic skin.

examples.title: Surfactant
examples.content: Soap breaks surface tension, causing pepper to scatter instantly.

slides.title: Tinkering with Solubility (Advanced)
slides.text:
Tinkering with salt, water, and sand shows the difference between solutions and mixtures.
This slide introduces key insights about Tinkering with Solubility.
For instance, consider the example of Solute & Solvent:
Salt (solute) dissolves in water (solvent) to form a uniform solution.
Another case is the study of Suspension:
Sand does not dissolve; it forms a mixture that settles over time.
How does the concept of Tinkering with Solubility help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Solute & Solvent
examples.content: Salt (solute) dissolves in water (solvent) to form a uniform solution.

examples.title: Suspension
examples.content: Sand does not dissolve; it forms a mixture that settles over time.

slides.title: Tinkering with Light Optics (Advanced)
slides.text:
Tinkering with water filled jars acts as cylinder lenses, refracting light to flip images.
This slide introduces key insights about Tinkering with Light Optics.
For instance, consider the example of Refraction Index:
Measure of how much a material bends light rays.
Another case is the study of Image Reversal:
Light rays cross at a focal point, reversing the image left to right.
How does the concept of Tinkering with Light Optics help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Refraction Index
examples.content: Measure of how much a material bends light rays.

examples.title: Image Reversal
examples.content: Light rays cross at a focal point, reversing the image left to right.

slides.title: Tinkering with Rotational Inertia (Advanced)
slides.text:
Tinkering with spinning eggs (raw vs.
hard-boiled) shows how internal states affect motion.
For instance, consider the example of Inertia:
An object's resistance to changes in its rotation state.
Another case is the study of Liquid Center:
Raw eggs spin poorly because the liquid sloshes around inside.
How does the concept of Tinkering with Rotational Inertia help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Inertia
examples.content: An object's resistance to changes in its rotation state.

examples.title: Liquid Center
examples.content: Raw eggs spin poorly because the liquid sloshes around inside.

slides.title: Tinkering with Light Reflection (Advanced)
slides.text:
Tinkering with multiple mirrors demonstrates how angle alignments create infinite reflections.
This slide introduces key insights about Tinkering with Light Reflection.
For instance, consider the example of Law of Reflection:
The angle of incidence equals the angle of reflection.
Another case is the study of Kaleidoscope:
Arranging mirrors at 60 degree angles creates hexagonal patterns.
How does the concept of Tinkering with Light Reflection help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Law of Reflection
examples.content: The angle of incidence equals the angle of reflection.

examples.title: Kaleidoscope
examples.content: Arranging mirrors at 60 degree angles creates hexagonal patterns.

slides.title: Tinkering with Pitch Frequency (Advanced)
slides.text:
Tinkering with glass bottles filled with different levels of water shows how air column length changes pitch.
This slide introduces key insights about Tinkering with Pitch Frequency.
For instance, consider the example of Resonance:
Striking a glass vibrates the water; blowing across the top vibrates the air.
Another case is the study of Wavelength:
More water means less air, creating shorter waves and higher pitches.
How does the concept of Tinkering with Pitch Frequency help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Resonance
examples.content: Striking a glass vibrates the water; blowing across the top vibrates the air.

examples.title: Wavelength
examples.content: More water means less air, creating shorter waves and higher pitches.

slides.title: Tinkering with Phototropism (Advanced)
slides.text:
Tinkering with cardboard shoe box mazes shows how plant seedlings navigate obstacles to find light.
This slide introduces key insights about Tinkering with Phototropism.
For instance, consider the example of Auxin:
Plant hormone that builds up on the dark side of a stem, causing it to bend.
Another case is the study of Adaptation:
Survival strategy ensuring leaves can perform photosynthesis.
How does the concept of Tinkering with Phototropism help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Auxin
examples.content: Plant hormone that builds up on the dark side of a stem, causing it to bend.

examples.title: Adaptation
examples.content: Survival strategy ensuring leaves can perform photosynthesis.

slides.title: Tinkering with Heat Conduction (Advanced)
slides.text:
Tinkering with butter pats placed on copper, plastic, and wooden spoons shows heat transfer rates.
This slide introduces key insights about Tinkering with Heat Conduction.
For instance, consider the example of Thermal Conductivity:
Measure of a material's ability to conduct heat energy.
Another case is the study of Observation:
Butter melts first on copper because metals are excellent conductors.
How does the concept of Tinkering with Heat Conduction help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Thermal Conductivity
examples.content: Measure of a material's ability to conduct heat energy.

examples.title: Observation
examples.content: Butter melts first on copper because metals are excellent conductors.

slides.title: Tinkering with Rock Density (Advanced)
slides.text:
Tinkering with a water overflow container allows us to measure the volume of irregular stones.
This slide introduces key insights about Tinkering with Rock Density.
For instance, consider the example of Displacement:
An object placed in water displaces a volume of water equal to its own volume.
Another case is the study of Density:
Mass divided by volume. Helps identify minerals.
How does the concept of Tinkering with Rock Density help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Displacement
examples.content: An object placed in water displaces a volume of water equal to its own volume.

examples.title: Density
examples.content: Mass divided by volume. Helps identify minerals.

slides.title: Tinkering with Seed Germination (Advanced)
slides.text:
Tinkering with soil acidity (adding vinegar or baking soda) tests chemical impact on seed growth.
This slide introduces key insights about Tinkering with Seed Germination.
For instance, consider the example of pH Level:
Measure of acidity or alkalinity of a growing medium.
Another case is the study of Germination Rate:
The percentage of seeds that sprout under specific test conditions.
How does the concept of Tinkering with Seed Germination help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: pH Level
examples.content: Measure of acidity or alkalinity of a growing medium.

examples.title: Germination Rate
examples.content: The percentage of seeds that sprout under specific test conditions.

slides.title: Guided Practice: Defining Questions (Advanced)
slides.text:
Refining a raw curiosity statement into a mathematically testable question.
This slide introduces key insights about Guided Practice: Defining Questions.
For instance, consider the example of Raw observation:
Toy cars go slower on rugs than on wooden floors.
Another case is the study of Testable Question:
How does the texture of a surface affect the friction force in Newtons?
How does the concept of Guided Practice: Defining Questions help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Raw observation
examples.content: Toy cars go slower on rugs than on wooden floors.

examples.title: Testable Question
examples.content: How does the texture of a surface affect the friction force in Newtons?

slides.title: Guided Practice: Controlled Setups (Advanced)
slides.text:
Designing a control group to compare results against experimental groups.
This slide introduces key insights about Guided Practice: Controlled Setups.
For instance, consider the example of Control Group:
The baseline setup kept under normal conditions (no changes).
Another case is the study of Experimental Group:
The setup where the independent variable is changed.
How does the concept of Guided Practice: Controlled Setups help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Control Group
examples.content: The baseline setup kept under normal conditions (no changes).

examples.title: Experimental Group
examples.content: The setup where the independent variable is changed.

slides.title: Guided Practice: Liquid Viscosity (Advanced)
slides.text:
Formulating questions about fluid dynamics and flow rates.
This slide introduces key insights about Guided Practice: Liquid Viscosity.
For instance, consider the example of Observation:
Cold maple syrup flows slower than warm maple syrup.
Another case is the study of Question:
How does liquid temperature affect viscosity and flow rate?
How does the concept of Guided Practice: Liquid Viscosity help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Observation
examples.content: Cold maple syrup flows slower than warm maple syrup.

examples.title: Question
examples.content: How does liquid temperature affect viscosity and flow rate?

slides.title: Guided Practice: Plant Geotropism (Advanced)
slides.text:
Formulating questions about gravity's impact on roots and stems.
This slide introduces key insights about Guided Practice: Plant Geotropism.
For instance, consider the example of Observation:
Roots grow downwards even if the seed is planted upside down.
Another case is the study of Question:
How does gravity influence the direction of root growth (geotropism)?
How does the concept of Guided Practice: Plant Geotropism help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Observation
examples.content: Roots grow downwards even if the seed is planted upside down.

examples.title: Question
examples.content: How does gravity influence the direction of root growth (geotropism)?

slides.title: Common Error: Confounding Variables (Advanced)
slides.text:
Changing more than one variable at a time makes it impossible to know which caused the result.
This slide introduces key insights about Common Error: Confounding Variables.
For instance, consider the example of Incorrect:
Testing different plane shapes using different paper materials.
Another case is the study of Correct:
Using the exact same paper material for all plane shapes tested.
How does the concept of Common Error: Confounding Variables help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Incorrect
examples.content: Testing different plane shapes using different paper materials.

examples.title: Correct
examples.content: Using the exact same paper material for all plane shapes tested.

slides.title: Common Error: Ignoring Anomalies (Advanced)
slides.text:
Do not ignore unexpected data points.
Anomalies often lead to new discoveries.
For instance, consider the example of Incorrect:
Deleting a test result because it does not fit your hypothesis.
Another case is the study of Correct:
Recording the anomaly and investigating what hidden variable caused it.
How does the concept of Common Error: Ignoring Anomalies help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Incorrect
examples.content: Deleting a test result because it does not fit your hypothesis.

examples.title: Correct
examples.content: Recording the anomaly and investigating what hidden variable caused it.

slides.title: Summary of Week 2 Advanced (Advanced)
slides.text:
You completed Week 2 Advanced! You can now isolate variables and formulate operational definitions.
This slide introduces key insights about Summary of Week 2 Advanced.
For instance, consider the example of Key Takeaway:
Tinkering must be done systematically by holding controls constant.
Another case is the study of Next Step:
Get ready to study advanced science tools and precision measurement in Week 3!
How does the concept of Summary of Week 2 Advanced help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Key Takeaway
examples.content: Tinkering must be done systematically by holding controls constant.

examples.title: Next Step
examples.content: Get ready to study advanced science tools and precision measurement in Week 3!

standard.q: Which of the following is the most testable scientific question?
options: Does heating honey reduce the time it takes to flow down a 30-degree ramp?

standard.q: What do we call the factor in an experiment that is intentionally changed by the scientist?
Why must we keep controlled variables constant in an experiment?
options: To make the experiment run faster.
To ensure that any changes in the dependent variable are caused ONLY by the independent variable.
To test multiple hypotheses at the same time.
To get a high score on the test.

standard.q: What property is demonstrated when water climbs up a paper towel suspended over a cup?
How does adding liquid dish soap to water affect its surface tension?
options: It reduces it, breaking the cohesive forces.

standard.q: A student wants to test if the number of coils in an electromagnet affects its strength. What is the dependent variable?
options: The number of coils wound around the nail

standard.q: Why does a raw egg spin slower and stop sooner than a hard-boiled egg?
options: The liquid inside the raw egg sloshes, creating internal friction (inertia) that opposes rotation.
The shell of a raw egg is rougher.
Hard-boiled eggs have more magic force.

standard.q: Which of the following describes Bernoulli's principle?
options: As fluid velocity increases, static pressure decreases.
Objects float if they displace equal water weight.
Action has an equal and opposite reaction.
Heat flows from hot to cold objects.

standard.q: In a plant shoe-box maze experiment testing phototropism, what is the independent variable?
options: The amount of light entering the box

standard.q: Which method is used to measure the volume of an irregular solid object like a stone?
options: Using a ruler to measure width
Water displacement in a graduated cylinder
Weighing it on a digital scale

standard.q: What is the angle of reflection if light hits a flat mirror at an angle of 30 degrees?
True or False: Viscosity is a liquid's resistance to flow.
What is a control group in a scientific experiment?
options: The group that controls the equipment.
The group kept under standard conditions to act as a baseline comparison.
The group where all variables are changed.

standard.q: Which variable is changed when testing the effect of salt concentration on water's boiling point?
What happens to water molecules at the surface to create surface tension?
options: They pull tightly towards each other due to cohesive forces.

standard.q: What hormone in plants is responsible for cell elongation on the dark side of a stem during phototropism?
Which material has the highest thermal conductivity?
How does increasing the frequency of a sound wave affect its pitch?
True or False: Capillary action occurs because water molecules adhere to the tube walls and cohere to each other.
What does pH measure in a soil sample?
Which question is non-testable because it contains subjective terms?
options: How many leaves does the plant grow in one week?
Is loam soil the best soil for growing roses?
Does clay soil hold more water than sandy soil?
What is the height of the plant after ten days?

standard.q: What is the density of a 100-gram rock that displaces 20 milliliters of water?
True or False: Changing both the type of soil and the amount of water in a plant test is a confounding setup.
What do we call unexpected data points that do not align with the hypothesis?
Which of the following is an operational definition of plant growth rate?
options: The change in plant height in centimeters per day
The quality of the fruit produced

challenge.q: You are testing if temperature affects the viscosity of oil. You measure the flow time in seconds. What is the dependent variable?
How would you set up a control group to test if a new plant food accelerates tomato production?
options: Grow one plant with double fertilizer.
Grow a group of identical plants under the exact same conditions, but with NO plant food added.
Grow different plant species with the food.

challenge.q: A student measures the displacement of a toy figurine in a cylinder. The water rises from 50 mL to 68 mL. What is the volume of the figurine?
Which of the following is a testable hypothesis based on the question: 'Does surface texture affect top speed?'
options: If the surface is rough, the top will spin slower due to friction.
Rough surfaces are very bad for spinning tops.
Circular force keeps tops standing upright.
Spinning tops is a fun science game.

challenge.q: What error is committed if a scientist rejects data points that contradict their hypothesis without investigation?

performance.title: Experimental Variable & Protocol Architect
performance.desc: Identify a advanced inquiry question (e.g., 'How does salt concentration affect the surface tension of water measured by the number of drops on a coin?'). Formulate a testable hypothesis, write down a step-by-step experimental protocol including a control group, and construct a blank data table to record your variables.
labels: Wrote a mathematically testable hypothesis with isolated variables
Detailed a logical experimental protocol with a clear control setup
Designed a professional data table with labeled units (e.g., mL, drops, seconds)

pages:
Exercise 1: Testable vs. Non-Testable Questions

Classify each scientific query as either Testable (can be measured and tested) or Non-Testable (subjective or vague).

1. Is clay the most fun material to play with in class?
Non-Testable
2. Does heating modeling clay reduce the force needed to bend it by 50%?
Testable
3. Do plants like music better than quiet rooms?
Non-Testable

Exercise 2: Identifying Confounding Variables

Read the scenario below and identify the mistake in the experimental design.

Scenario: Matteo wants to test if wing size affects paper plane distance. He folds a small-winged plane using heavy cardboard and a large-winged plane using thin copy paper. He throws them both in the wind.

Identify the confounding variables that make this test unfair:
Matteo changed both the wing size and the paper thickness/weight,
making it impossible to know which factor affected the flight distance.

Exercise 3: Advanced Variable Control Protocol

Write down the operational definition and the baseline control for the following question.

Question: How does water salinity affect capillary action in cotton fabric?
1. Operational Definition of Capillary Action:
The height in millimeters that liquid climbs up a fabric strip in 5 minutes
2. Baseline Control Group:
A cotton fabric strip placed in pure tap water with zero salt added
